1.description: Sample Form ADV: Renaissance Technologies Notes. (a) Section 1 , Form ADV. (b) Section 7.B.(1), Fund Identity, Form ADV. (c) Section 7.B.(1), Ownership Reporting, Form ADV. This figure shows three excerpts from the SEC’s Form ADV for a sample firm, Renaissance Technologies LLC. (a) Basic information to identify firms. (b) Basic fund information for our sample fund, Medallion Fund L.P., and is found in Section 7.B.(1). (c) Ownership data such as minimum investment, number of investors, and basic composition of investors and is reported at the fund level. We rely primarily on question 14, at the fund level, when studying insider ownership. Form ADVs can be searched at https://www.adviserinfo.sec.gov/

2.description: Decomposition of Earnings for Hedge Fund Managers Notes. This figure distinguishes between three possible sources of hedge fund earnings—managerial fees (blue), performance fees (green), and excess returns on privately invested capital (red). The sample is restricted to funds for which all fee data are available, and the sample excludes eVestment (for which we do not have accurate assets under management data). To compute management fees, we multiply listed management fees for each fund against monthly assets under management invested by outside investors (assuming that insiders do not pay fees). Performance fees are computed assuming that all funds operate under a high-water mark that begins the first month for which we observe each fund’s performance. Stated performance fees are multiplied by the total raw return (assuming no hurdle rate) on outsider fund investment. Insider returns are calculated based on each fund’s excess return from a Fung-Hsieh Seven-Factor model multiplied by the quantity of inside investment in place within each fund.

3.description: Merge Details Notes. (a) Among ADV hedge funds, merge rate into commercial data sets. (b) Merge rate by commercial data provider. This figure plots the merge rate between observations in Form ADV and those in hedge fund commercial return data sets (outlined in the data section). All data are taken as of 2015. (a) Fraction of observations in the ADV data set for which we find a merged counterpart in the commercial return data sets, with the fraction reported for each five-point interval of inside investment. (b) Merge rate as a fraction of the total funds listed in each of the commercial hedge fund data providers. The order of the data sets reflects the sequential match process—we first search for matches in HFR, then BarclayHedge, then eVestment, then CISDM, then Eureka. For this reason, the match rate for each data set reflects a conditional match rate, given that the fund did not merge in the previous data set, and so our merge rates generally decrease as we move across providers.

4.description: Distribution of Insider Investment from Merged Sample Notes. (a) Distribution of insider investment across funds, percentage of total assets. (b) Distribution of insider investment across funds, gross inside investment. This figure plots insider investment into hedge funds from the merged sample of hedge fund returns and ADV forms. (a) Histogram of insider investment, with units of percent of total investment. Insider investment displays a “dumbbell” distribution. (b) Histogram of log(Gross Asset Value) of insider investment across funds for the merged sample between ADV and the commercial hedge fund data sets.

5.description: Insider Investment Allocation by Fund Scale Notes. (a) Scale and strategy. (b) Scale and liquidity. This figure plots the coefficients from a regression estimating a decreasing returns to scale parameter interacted with an indicator for high investment funds. The lines represent 95% confidence intervals. The specification, following Yin (2016) , regresses measures of excess return against a number of covariates: E x c e s s R e t u r n 𝑖 , 𝑡 = 𝛽 ⁢ l o g ⁡ ( 𝐴 𝑈 𝑀 𝑖 , 𝑡 − 1 ) × H i g h O w n e r s h i p 𝑖 , 𝑡 − 1 + c o n t r o l s + 𝜀 𝑖 , 𝑡 ExcessReturn i , t = β ⁢ log ⁡ ( AUM i , t − 1 ) × HighOwnership i , t − 1 + controls + ε i , t . Measures of excess return include the return in excess of the style average (following Yin 2016 ) and Fung-Hsieh factor models. Controls include log( 𝐴 𝑈 𝑀 𝑡 − 1 AUM t − 1 ), log(total firm 𝐴 𝑈 𝑀 𝑡 − 1 AUM t − 1 ), percent AUM 𝐹 𝑙 𝑜 𝑤 ( 𝑡 − 1 ) → 𝑡 Flow ( t − 1 ) → t , performance fee, management fee, highwater mark, log(level of minimum investment), redemption period, fund age, whether the fund is open to new investment, and leverage. Further, we include fixed effects for the firm, year, and strategy, clustering at the fund level. All flow and return measures are winsorized at the 1% level. The key parameter plotted is β , which captures the extent to which high insider funds experience greater dilution of returns when more capital is deployed, consistent with returns to scale which are more decreasing. (a) Scale parameter across a variety of fund strategies. (b) Two different measures of liquidity: (1) funds with a lower than median Getmansky et al. (2004) measure, indicating more illiquid funds and (2) funds with a higher than median number of redemption days, again intended to capture more illiquid funds.

6.description: Flow-Performance Relationship of Funds, Split by Insider Fund Status Notes. This figure plots the density of the relationship between lagged excess return and contemporaneous flow, measured quarterly. The horizontal axis corresponds to lagged quarterly excess returns. Excess returns are defined using the Fung-Hsieh model. The vertical axis corresponds to the current quarter’s fund flow, as a percent of lagged AUM. The flow measure is defined as 𝐹 𝑙 𝑜 𝑤 𝑖 ⁢ 𝑡 = 𝐴 𝑈 𝑀 𝑖 ⁢ 𝑡 − ( 1 + 𝑟 𝑖 ⁢ 𝑡 ) · 𝐴 𝑈 𝑀 𝑖 , 𝑡 − 1 𝐴 𝑈 𝑀 𝑖 , 𝑡 − 1 Flow i ⁢ t = AUM i ⁢ t − ( 1 + r i ⁢ t ) · AUM i , t − 1 AUM i , t − 1 . Flows are winsorized at a 1% level. The green line corresponds to outsider funds, while the red line corresponds to insider funds. Both lines are estimated using a local weighted regression (loess) method. Insider and outsider funds are defined by the median level of inside investment into insider funds.

7.description: Cumulative Returns by Portfolios Sorted on Inside Investment Notes. This figure plots returns to the first and tenth decile portfolios sorted based on the level of inside investment for each fund over the period 2011–2016Q2. The horizontal axis corresponds to time in years. The vertical axis corresponds to returns. Each line tracks the cumulative return for each decile portfolio. The portfolios are reformulated each year based on the reported percent of inside investment. Decile 1, the dotted red line, corresponds to funds with the lowest amount of inside investment. Decile 10, the solid green line, corresponds to the highest level of inside investment. Returns for each portfolio are computed by taking a weighted average by assets of the fund.

8.description: Cumulative Returns by Portfolios Sorted on Inside Investment: Within Firm Notes. This figure plots the high-minus-low spread portfolio excess return based on inside investment, within a fund family. Excess returns are estimated using the Fungh-Hsieh factor approach. The horizontal axis corresponds to time in years. The vertical axis corresponds to percent return. The trading strategy studies funds with greater than one fund within the family. Funds with the highest percent of inside investment, within the family, are long, while funds with the lowest of percent inside investment, within a family, are short. The portfolios are reformed annually to coincide with the periodicity of ADV filings. We estimate a Fung and Hsieh alpha of 17 basis points a month using our spread portfolio.

9.description: Event Study, Transition from One Fund to Multiple Funds, Within a Firm Notes. This figure plots the net cumulative returns of a firm which launches an additional fund after previously only having one. Event times correspond to months from the fund creation date. The lines plot the cumulative performance of the original fund, comparing across two situations. When the newly created fund has less inside investment than the original, we say that the original fund is “high inside investment” (in red). When the newly created fund has higher inside capital than the original, the original fund has “low inside investment” (green) In both cases, we track the performance of the original fund across the two scenarios. The red line’s postfund creation rise in returns indicates that fund performance improves when the newly created fund vehicle has less inside investment than the original. In a difference-in-difference regression, the interacted term of High Inside × Post has a coefficient of 0.969 and a standard error of 0.214, which is significant at the 1% level.

10.description: Survival Plot for High and Low Inside Investment Funds Notes. This figure plots Kaplan-Meier estimates of the survival rates for funds with high and low levels of inside ownership. Green lines represent estimates for funds with high average inside investment, whereas red lines represent funds with low inside investment. The horizontal axis corresponds to time in years from the first year observed in the Form ADV data. The vertical axis corresponds to survival rates. High levels of inside investment is determined by higher than sample median based on all ADV filings of hedge funds. Closures of funds are not always observed, we proxy this by the final date the fund reports in ADV. If the fund reports in the final year of our sample, we record this as surviving but right censored.

11.description: Inside Investment Return Relationship by Fund Type Notes. (a) Effects by fund type. (b) Effects by fund type among equity funds. This figure presents a robustness analysis of the specification in column 1 of Panel A of Table 9 , which is a panel regression of inside investment against factor-corrected returns. This figure plots the coefficient on inside investment, corresponding to the predictive value of that variable on excess returns, run in a separate regression for each fund category. Funds are categorized based on descriptions found in commercial hedge fund data sets, as listed in the Data section. The error bars indicate a 95% confidence interval for the estimated coefficients.
